records.ety: [L. dilapidare to scatter like stones; di- = dis- + lapidare to throw stones, fr. lapis a stone. See Lapidary.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Dilapidated (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Dilapidating (?).]

defs: To bring into a condition of decay or partial ruin, by misuse or through neglect; to destroy the fairness and good condition of; -- said of a building.
quotes: If the bishop, parson, or vicar, etc., dilapidates the buildings, or cuts down the timber of the patrimony.

defs: To impair by waste and abuse; to squander.
quotes: The patrimony of the bishopric of Oxon was much dilapidated.

defs: To get out of repair; to fall into partial ruin; to become decayed; as, the church was suffered to dilapidate.
